region_of_origin: Global (freshwater fish found across Asia, North America, Africa, and Europe)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for raw catfish; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw catfish is one fillet weighing approximately 150 g, providing around 239 kcal, 27.6 g protein, and 12.9 g fat before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Catfish, channel, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 15011)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macros (energy 159 kcal, protein 18.4 g, fat 8.6 g, sodium 46 mg, potassium 320 mg, calcium 18 mg, iron 0.4 mg, cholesterol 73 mg) are preserved from the HPB Singapore Foodo record. Carbohydrate is reported as -1 (not available) in the HPB source; USDA SR Legacy reports 0.0 g carbohydrate for raw channel catfish, which is consistent with the negligible-carbohydrate nature of this food. Saturated fat (2.05 g), monounsaturated fat (2.07 g), polyunsaturated fat (2.05 g), sugar (0.0 g), and additional micronutrients are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15011 as a complementary approved source.

gi_source.title: N/A — Catfish, raw contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Catfish, raw; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g per 100 g). No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. glycemic_index set to -1 (sentinel for not available / not applicable) consistent with numeric hygiene rules; gi_category is 'not_applicable'.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (46 mg/100 g) and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (320 mg potassium vs 46 mg sodium)
hypertension.guidance: Raw catfish is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its low sodium content (46 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium level (320 mg/100 g) support blood pressure regulation, provided it is prepared without added salt, high-sodium sauces, or deep-frying.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (0.0 g/100 g) and high protein (18.4 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw catfish is an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its negligible carbohydrate content produces no postprandial glucose rise, and its high protein content (18.4 g/100 g) promotes satiety and supports lean muscle mass without compromising glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA/DHA), low saturated fat (2.05 g/100 g), and moderate cholesterol (73 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw catfish provides omega-3 fatty acids (approximately 2.05 g polyunsaturated fat/100 g including EPA and DHA) that support endothelial function and reduce triglycerides; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer grilling or steaming over deep-frying to avoid adding atherogenic trans fats and excess saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) with anti-inflammatory prostaglandin-modulating properties and low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw catfish is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its omega-3 fatty acid content, which modulates prostaglandin pathways to reduce joint inflammation; grilling or steaming is preferred over deep-frying, which generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may worsen joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Catfish (raw) is a freshwater fish characterised by smooth, scaleless skin, white to light-pink flesh, and a firm, moist texture. It is widely consumed across Southeast Asia, North America, and Africa, and is valued for its mild, slightly sweet flavour that adapts well to grilling, steaming, frying, and braising. Nutritionally, raw catfish is a high-protein, moderate-fat food providing approximately 159 kcal, 18.4 g protein, and 8.6 g fat per 100 g. It contains negligible carbohydrates, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Its fat profile includes omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which confer anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective benefits. Sodium content is low at 46 mg/100 g, and potassium is present at 320 mg/100 g, supporting a favourable sodium-potassium ratio beneficial for blood pressure management. Catfish is an excellent dietary choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is considered halal. In Singapore and Malaysia, catfish is commonly known as ikan keli and is used in spiced curries and grilled preparations. The glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content.
common_user_queries: Is raw catfish high in protein?
Is catfish good for high blood pressure?
Is catfish suitable for heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Carbohydrate is confirmed as negligible (0.0 g) consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for raw channel catfish. Saturated fat, fatty acid fractions, and micronutrients are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15011), an approved primary source. The food identity is unambiguous and the nutrient profile is internally consistent.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g: The input HPB record reported carbohydrate as -1 (data not available). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15011) reports 0.0 g carbohydrate for raw channel catfish, consistent with the negligible-carbohydrate nature of all raw fish. The value has been set to 0.0 g and is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 2. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as catfish is an animal-origin food containing no plant fibre. 3. sugar_g is naturally zero as raw fish contains no free sugars. 4. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw catfish. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.07 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.05 g), saturated_fat_g (2.05 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15011 as the HPB record did not report fatty acid fractions. 6. glycemic_index is set to -1 (numeric sentinel for not available / not applicable) because catfish contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. This satisfies the q4 numeric requirement while correctly representing the inapplicability of GI for this food. 7. The rheumatoid_arthritis entry in the input had an internally contradictory suitable=false flag combined with a reason citing omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefits; this has been corrected to suitable=true (low concern) with accurate clinical guidance. 8. SNOMED CT code not assigned; no confirmed SNOMED CT food concept for raw catfish was identified in publicly available SNOMED CT browser as of 2026-04-16.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Catfish, raw (F03010011) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1-Q8 scorecard. The key fix in iteration 3 was changing glycemic_index from null to -1 (numeric sentinel for not applicable) to satisfy the q4 validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. Core macros are complete and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15011 (complementary). The input carbohydrate sentinel value of -1 was resolved to 0.0 g with USDA confirmation and documented in notes. All three mandatory health conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) are present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology anchors (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are defensible and honest. Alias coverage includes English variants and Malay regional names. LLM description exceeds 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context. Final decision: PASS.
